name: loglady
documentation:
# Loglady

[![PyPI - Version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/loglady.svg)](https://pypi.org/project/loglady)
[![PyPI - Python Version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/loglady.svg)](https://pypi.org/project/loglady)

-----

**Table of Contents**

- [Installation](#installation)
- [License](#license)

## Installation

```console
pip install loglady
```

## License

`loglady` is distributed under the terms of the [MIT](https://spdx.org/licenses/MIT.html) license.

modules.name: loglady._config_stack
documentation:
Global state stack management.

LogLady's magics (the top-level loglady.info, etc.) need to have access to the
manager created from calling configure(), this manages keeping track of that
global state. It's done as a stack to make life easier for tests, as this allows
multiple calls to configure() to not stomp on each other.
Copyright (c) 2024 Alethea Katherine Flowers.
Published under the standard MIT License.
Full text available at: https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT

functions.name: loglady._config_stack.top
documentation:
Returns the currently active global config or None if LogLady hasn't
been configured.

functions.name: loglady._config_stack.push
documentation: Add the given state to the top of the stack, making it active.

functions.name: loglady._config_stack.pop
documentation:
Remove the currently active global config from the stack and return it.

Returns None if the stack is empty.

functions.name: loglady._config_stack.rewind
documentation:
A context manager that automatically rewinds the stack on exit.

This is useful for applying temporary configuration in tests and such.

class_variables.name: loglady._config_stack.GlobalState.manager
documentation: The manager created via configure() or configure_for_tests()

class_variables.name: loglady._config_stack.GlobalState.logger
documentation: The root logger used by magics

modules.name: loglady.manager
documentation: Copyright (c) 2024 Alethea Katherine Flowers.
Published under the standard MIT License.
Full text available at: https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT

classes.name: loglady.manager.Manager

methods.name: loglady.manager.Manager.logger
documentation: Get a new Logger

methods.name: loglady.manager.Manager.start
documentation: Tell the transport to start

methods.name: loglady.manager.Manager.stop
documentation: Flush all destinations and stop the transport

methods.name: loglady.manager.Manager.flush
documentation: Ask all destinations to write any pending logs

methods.name: loglady.manager.Manager.relay
documentation: Run a record through middleware and hand it off to the transport

Wrangles all the bits of LogLady so that things work!

Managers can be constructed directly, though typically you'll use
loglady.configure() so that it gets globally registered as the active
manager.

A manager gives a Logger life: Any records created by a Logger are sent to
the manager. The manager runs the record through middleware and then hands
it off to the transport. The transport is responsible for sending the record
to all destinations.

Managers are responsible for the lifetime of its Transport and Destinations.
If you're manually creating managers, don't forget to call start() and
stop().

A destination is responsible for *outputting* a Record. They're the last link in the chain.

classes.name: loglady.logger.Logger

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.__init__
documentation:
Loggers shouldn't be created directly, instead, use loglady.configure()
and loglady.logger() to get an instance.

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.context
documentation:
A read-only view of the current context. Use bind() or unbind() to
change the context.

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.bind
documentation:
Create a new logger with the given context. The new logger inherits
this logger's context.

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.unbind
documentation: Create a new logger without the given keys in the context.

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.prefix
documentation: Shortcut for self.bind(prefix="...", ...)

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.log
documentation:
You probably don't wanna call this, as it's the common log method
used by info(), warning(), etc. I mean, you can call it, I'm a
docstring, not a cop.

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.trace
documentation:
Log a debug message and include a stack trace.

Note that middleware.add_exception_and_stack_info or a similar
middleware must be used otherwise it won't actually add the stack info.

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.debug
documentation: Log a debug message

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.info
documentation: Log an info message

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.warning
documentation: Log a warning message

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.success
documentation: Log a success message

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.error
documentation: Log an error message

methods.name: loglady.logger.Logger.exception
documentation:
Log an error message and include the current exception's traceback

Note that middleware.add_exception_and_stack_info or a similar
middleware must be used otherwise it won't actually add the exception
and traceback.
It's the logger! You know how to log!

modules.name: loglady.rich._stacktrace
documentation:
Rich-compatible stacktrace printer.

Rich doesn't have a way to nicely print stacktraces without an Exception involved,
so this module allows for log.trace() to print out the stacktrace.

It's a lot of code. It's mostly because Thea can't leave well enough alone.
Copyright (c) 2024 Alethea Katherine Flowers.
Published under the standard MIT License.
Full text available at: https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT

functions.name: loglady.rich._stacktrace._make_theme

modules.name: loglady.magics
documentation:
Magics are sugary convenience methods to quickly and easily log to your
heart's content.

These are exposed as top-level methods on the loglady module:

    import loglady

    loglady.configure()

    loglady.info("Hello!")

Magics all work as long as configure() has been called. They match the methods
found on Logger.
Copyright (c) 2024 Alethea Katherine Flowers.
Published under the standard MIT License.
Full text available at: https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT

Full text available at: https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT

classes.name: loglady.transport.Transport
documentation: Transports are responsible for relaying records to a list of destinations

classes.name: loglady.transport.SyncTransport
documentation:
A very simple transport that immediately relays records to destinations.

This is useful for tests but not recommended for real applications.

classes.name: loglady.transport.ThreadedTransport
documentation:
A transport that handles relaying in a separate thread.

This prevents logging calls from blocking, as they only need to queue the
record.

functions.name: loglady._tracebackhide.check_for_tracebackhide
documentation:
Helper for ignoring stackframes from functions that have a variable
named __stacktracehide__ defined.

This is adapted from pytest's TracebackEntry.ishidden

modules.name: loglady.config
documentation:
Global configuration for LogLady.

This global config is used by magics (the top-level loglady.info, etc.), and
should be configured at application startup.
Copyright (c) 2024 Alethea Katherine Flowers.
Published under the standard MIT License.
Full text available at: https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT

functions.name: loglady.config.configure
documentation:
Configure LogLady.

This creates global configuration for LogLady that's then available via the
top-level loglady.info(...), loglady.logger(...), etc. methods (these are
called "magics").

This creates a Manager instance and start()s it so that any background
stuff can happen. It also installs an atexit() handler to call the Manager's
stop() to ensure all logs are written before exit.

functions.name: loglady.config.manager
documentation: Returns the currently configured Manager, or None if not configured.

functions.name: loglady.middleware.add_thread_info
documentation: Adds teh current thread native id and name

functions.name: loglady.middleware.add_exception_and_stack_info
documentation:
Adds the current exception info and stacktrace

The exception info is added if record["exc_info"] is True, likewise,
the stacktrace is added if record["stack_info"] is True.

functions.name: loglady.middleware.add_call_info
documentation: Add the calling function's name, filename, module, and lineno

functions.name: loglady.middleware._find_app_frame
documentation: Finds the first frame that isn't part of the logging code

classes.name: loglady.pytest.LogladyPlugin

methods.name: loglady.pytest.LogladyPlugin.pytest_runtest_setup
documentation: Hooks

Plugin for pytest that gathers global logs and associates them with tests.

This is intended to function like pytest's build-in capsys and caplog features.

Refs:
- https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/72c682ff9773ad2690711105a100423ebf7c7c15/src/_pytest/capture.py#L709-L710
